text: Negative split pacing (running the second half faster than the first) improves energy conservation and glycogen sparing during marathon racing.
Negative split pacing enhances thermoregulation and reduces cardiovascular drift compared to even or positive split strategies.
Negative split pacing delays fatigue and improves efficiency in the latter stages of a marathon.
Progressive long runs and fast-finish workouts are effective training modalities for developing the ability to execute negative split pacing in races.
Psychological techniques such as goal setting and visualization can help runners implement negative split race strategies.
Negative split pacing strategy should be individualized based on fitness level, environmental conditions, and race course profile rather than applied universally.
